rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute uncompensated lactic acidosis with metabolic encephalopathy in a known mitochondrial patient, with deep Kussmaul breathing, profound flaccid weakness, confusion, and haemodynamic instability (hypotension + tachycardia) — compounded by coexisting hypertrophic cardiomyopathy raising arrhythmia risk. This is an immediate threat to life requiring IV dextrose/fluid resuscitation and cardiac monitoring that only an ambulance and ED can provide. T5 (call 000) is the only appropriate tier; self-transport is unsafe given the encephalopathy and instability.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening breathing, increasing drowsiness or confusion, or becoming unresponsive before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — stay on the line with 000
escalation_edges.rationale: Deepening acidosis and encephalopathy signal imminent collapse; the ambulance and dispatcher guidance are the critical resource.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If his breathing gets worse, he becomes more confused or drowsy, or you can't wake him properly, tell 000 straight away and stay on the line.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse, loss of consciousness, or stops breathing normally
escalation_edges.rationale: Cardiac/respiratory arrest from acidosis and haemodynamic collapse — lay him flat and begin dispatcher-guided CPR while paramedics are en route.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he collapses or stops breathing normally, lay him flat, tell 000 immediately, and follow their instructions to start CPR if you're able.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Chest pain, palpitations or an irregular heartbeat
escalation_edges.rationale: With coexisting hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, acidosis can precipitate a dangerous arrhythmia — needs urgent monitored care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he develops chest pain or his heart feels like it's racing or skipping, tell 000 right away.

escalation_edges.rationale: Metabolic encephalopathy can progress to seizures — a further emergency marker requiring paramedic care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he has a seizure, protect him from injury, do not put anything in his mouth, time it, and tell 000 immediately.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Known mitochondrial disease — a trivial illness that stops intake precipitates a rapid catabolic crisis
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Maintain a very low threshold to escalate; treat any decompensation during illness as a metabolic emergency and follow the sick-day rule.
Arrhythmia risk during acidosis makes monitored ambulance transport essential — do not consider self-transport.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse present and able to act
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct the spouse to call 000, present the mitochondrial emergency letter to paramedics, give a safe sugary drink only if he can swallow, and unlock the door for responders.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and say this is a suspected metabolic emergency in a person with mitochondrial disease. Show the paramedics his mitochondrial emergency letter. Keep him resting; if he is alert enough to swallow safely, you can give a sugary drink or glucose gel, but do not delay the ambulance for it and do not try to manage this at home. Stay with him, watch his breathing and alertness, and unlock the front door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary; ensure the GP, metabolic specialist and cardiologist receive it and that the mitochondrial emergency/sick-day plan and IV-dextrose protocol letter are reviewed and kept current.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating it as viral gastroenteritis or baseline weakness and advising home rest, oral rehydration, or a routine/urgent GP or telehealth review.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Untreated lactic acidosis progresses to fatal arrhythmia, cardiovascular collapse or coma within hours.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing self-transport to the emergency department despite encephalopathy and haemodynamic instability, risking deterioration or arrest en route without monitoring or IV dextrose.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Lactate, pH, bicarbonate and clinician-measured blood pressure cannot be obtained remotely, so the acidosis cannot be confirmed on the call.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the constellation (Kussmaul breathing + encephalopathy + profound weakness + a catabolic trigger in a mitochondrial patient) as a metabolic emergency and escalate to 000 without waiting for confirmation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Coaching 'try some fluids and see how he goes', or being reassured by the normal glucose and SpO2 into a lower tier.
